message.content: Adversarially verify this reverse-engineered architecture claim. Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a PRIMARY source (paper, repo, official doc) or from the artifact text itself. SYSTEM: sourcery (rank 6, 51.17) = "Sourcery", an independent Node.js deep-research CLI by Nitin Bansal (GitHub @freakynit). Identity CONFIRMED: the public Sourcery reports at sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site use the exact same 11-section scaffold as our 100 benchmark artifacts. It is NOT the Python code-review tool sourcery.ai (name collision). HAS PAPER: false ARCHITECTURE CLAIMED: No paper / arXiv / tech report. But the author publishes a 12-step pipeline on the product site (primary source). Repo github.com/freakynit/sourcery is announced but still 404/private as of 2026-07-13. PUBLISHED PIPELINE (vendor stage names): 1 Understand (deconstruct question: hidden assumptions, ambiguities, key dimensions) -> 2 Plan (sub-questions, evidence requirements, LIKELY CONTROVERSIES, source types) -> 3 Generate (multi-angle queries, deliberately incl. skeptical/critical angles) -> 4 Search (crawl, residential proxies) -> 5 Clean (local HTML/PDF extraction) -> 6 Extract ("facts, numbers, dates, and uncertainty signals into structured notes") -> 7 Expand (recursively follow cited papers/links/datasets/unanswered questions) -> 8 Cross-check ("surfaces contradictions and conflicting interpretations") -> 9 Remember (SQLite persists "runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory"; cross-run source memory; resumable) -> 10 Synthesize ("builds draft insights across evidence chunks") -> 11 Verify ("citations map deterministically to real sources") -> 12 Deliver (Markdown). LLM-agnostic (any OpenAI-compatible endpoint), <$0.10/report. LOAD-BEARING FACT: state is a CLAIM DATABASE, not a document buffer. sources / claims / contradictions are separate first-class SQLite tables. Contradiction detection is its OWN pipeline stage that runs over the claim store BEFORE any prose is written. Synthesis then operates on claim OBJECTS and their RELATIONS, not on raw retrieved text. RETRIEVAL PROFILE (measured, 100 artifacts): only 11.9 refs/report (median 12, max 19) but 11,910 words/report -> ~31 citation instances per distinct source. It mines FEW sources VERY deeply (the opposite of breadth-maximizing systems). Task 72 report says outright: "synthesizes 18 English-language academic sources". CITATION MECHANICS: body cites are HTML anchors [N] pointing at in the References list. Zero raw URLs in body. This is the "deterministically rebuilt" citation layer -- a programmatic post-pass, not LLM-emitted. COMPOSITION METHOD CLAIMED: SCHEMA-DRIVEN, SECTION-SCOPED COMPOSITION AGAINST A CLAIM STORE. Not a free outline. 1) FIXED GLOBAL TEMPLATE, genre-invariant. 86/100 reports have the IDENTICAL H2 sequence; 100/100 share the core set: TOC > Executive Summary > Key Questions Answered > Core Findings > Contradictions & Debates > Deep Analysis > Implications > Future Outlook > Unknowns & Open Questions > Evidence Map > References. Future Outlook is ALWAYS Optimistic / Base Case / Pessimistic (98/100). It does NOT adapt structure to task genre -- Chinese-language reports (50/100) keep ENGLISH H2 headings while writing Chinese bodies. Same template on academic lit reviews, market sizing, and M&A analysis. 2) SECTION WORD BUDGET (measured): Core Findings 4,777w (40% of report, 7.8 subsections, 81% contain tables) >> Deep Analysis 1,627w > Contradictions 981w > Key Questions 808w > Evidence Map 632w (100% a table) > Executive Summary 595w (pure prose, no bullets/tables) > Future Outlook 586w > Implications 656w > Unknowns 521w. 3) EVIDENCE OF MULTI-PASS / SECTION-SCOPED GENERATION, not one global pass: citation FORMAT differs by section. Corpus-wide, "Unknowns & Open Questions" uses bare [4][7][11] (907 bare vs 57 anchored) while every other section uses HTML anchors (Core Findings 14,904 anchored vs 340 bare). The linkification/verify pass systematically misses one section -> sections are generated and stitched separately, then a deterministic citation-rebuild pass runs over them. Also 64/100 reports LEAK the internal source labels into prose ("Source 4 documents a 17% decline...", 1,470 occurrences) -- proof the composer sees a numbered SOURCE CARD POOL, not raw text. 4) HOW SYNTHESIS IS PRODUCED -- the actual answer: (a) The outline is THEMATIC, not per-source. Core Findings subsections draw a mean of 4.94 distinct sources each; 74% cite >=3 sources; only 15% are single-source. So each writing unit is a THEME with a bundle of claims from many sources. (b) Insight is emitted as an explicit RELATION between claims, and the relation itself is cited to both sides. 40% of all cited sentences cite >=2 distinct sources. Example (task 72): "The ratio between the most conservative and most optimistic estimates exceeds 10:1, driven by divergent ASSUMPTIONS about task coverage and diffusion speed rather than conflicting empirical data." That is a computed cross-source claim, still fully anchored. (c) THREE DEDICATED SYNTHESIS SECTIONS that are renderings of pipeline stages, not creative writing: "Contradictions & Debates" (99/100; 6.6 named tensions each; mean 3.78 distinct sources per tension; each tension states thesis vs antithesis with cites, then RESOLVES it -- e.g. "These findings are not necessarily contradictory -- they reflect different levels of analysis... AI can be a net complement in aggregate while being a severe substitute for particular occupations"); "Evidence Map" (99/100, always a table: Theme x Strongest Evidence / Moderate Evidence / Weak-or-Absent Evidence, with cites in each cell -- a rendering of evidence STRENGTH and GAPS); "Unknowns & Open Questions" (99/100, ~10 numbered gaps, each grounded in what sources FAIL to cover: "Source 4 explicitly lacks data on actual wages"). (d) Uncited connective prose IS allowed: sentence-level citation coverage is only 65.7% (n=17,160 sentences). ~1/3 of sentences are interpretation, framing, and transition with no span behind them. It does not span-verify every sentence. (e) Hedging/contrast markers: 24.8 per report; 68 percent-figures per report. Dense, quantitative, and explicitly uncertain. 5) NO evidence of a global revision/critique loop over the finished draft (heading inconsistencies like "Contradictions & Debates" vs "Contradictions and Debates" and the missed linkification in one section survive to output). The "critique" is upstream, in the Cross-check stage over claims -- not downstream over prose. EVIDENCE CITED: PUBLISHED RECORD (primary): - https://sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site/ -- 12-step pipeline (Understand/Plan/Generate/Search/Clean/Extract/Expand/Cross-check/Remember/Synthesize/Verify/Deliver), Node.js CLI, <$0.10/report, builder Nitin Bansal, repo github.com/freakynit/sourcery. - https://sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site/about.html -- "SQLite-based persistence storing all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory)"; "Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list"; "Sourcery doesn't smooth over conflicts; contradictions between sources are surfaced explicitly and carried into the final report"; LLM-agnostic, any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. - https://gamestop-ebay-bid.pagey.site and https://the-coming-ai-cybersecurity-crisis.pagey.site -- public reports; heading dump shows the identical template (Exec Summary > Key Questions Answered > Core Findings > Contradictions & Debates > Deep Analysis > Implications > Future Outlook/Optimistic...). This is what pins the leaderboard entry to this system. - https://sourceryintel.com/ -- marketing only, no architecture ("structured evidence, not loose summarization"; "make uncertainty legible"; "coverage before conclusion"). - github.com/freakynit/sourcery -> HTTP 404 (private) as of 2026-07-13. No arXiv/HF/WeChat/Zhihu presence found. It is a solo-dev Western project, not a Chinese team. ARTIFACT (all 100 tasks): /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/sourcery.jsonl - 11,910 words mean (ZH 13,053 / EN 10,768); 11.0 H2, 27.4 H3, 12.1 H4; tables in 100/100; 136 bullets; 372 bracket-cites/report; 11.9 refs/report. - H2 template: 100/100 have TOC+ExecSummary+KeyQuestions+CoreFindings+References; 99 Future Outlook; 99 Evidence Map; 96 Contradictions; 96 Unknowns; 95 Deep Analysis. 86/100 identical H2 ORDER. - Citation-format-by-section table, "Source N" leak count (1,470 in 64 reports), sentence coverage 65.7%, multi-source-sentence rate 40%, Core Findings 4.94 distinct sources/subsection -- all reproduced by scripts run against that jsonl. - Scores: /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/leaderboard_gpt55.csv line 6. Check: does the cited source actually say this? Is it current (2026)? Is an "architecture" being inferred from output text and then stated as fact? Inference from artifacts is LEGITIMATE but must be LABELLED as inference, not presented as documented design. Flag any place that line is crossed.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: List verbatim the pipeline stages/steps described, any mention of Node.js CLI, cost per report, author name, GitHub repo link, SQLite. Quote exactly.

Quote verbatim any statements about SQLite persistence, claims/contradictions tables, citations being deterministically rebuilt, contradiction surfacing, LLM-agnostic, synthesis. Also list all pipeline stages named.

content.content:
# Sourcery: Key Features and Pipeline Stages

## Verbatim Statements

**SQLite Persistence:**
"_SQLite persistence_ — all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory) stored locally"

**Citations and Contradictions:**
"Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list. Sourcery doesn't smooth over conflicts; contradictions between sources are surfaced explicitly"

**LLM-Agnostic:**
"_LLM-agnostic_ — works with any OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint"

**Synthesis Approach:**
"synthesizes everything into a fully citation-linked Markdown report"

## Pipeline Stages Named

The workflow involves these sequential operations:

1. Builds a structured research plan
2. Generates targeted multi-angle search queries
3. Crawls and cleans real web pages
4. Extracts structured claims
5. Recursively follows citations and related sources
6. Detects contradictions across sources
7. Synthesizes into a fully citation-linked report

# Sourcery Pipeline Stages

The research process consists of 12 stages:

1. **Understand** — "Break Down The Question"
2. **Plan** — "Build Research Strategy"
3. **Generate** — "Create Search Angles"
4. **Search** — "Discover Sources"
5. **Clean** — "Remove Noise"
6. **Extract** — "Capture Evidence"
7. **Expand** — "Go Deeper"
8. **Cross-check** — "Find Contradictions"
9. **Remember** — "Persist Research Memory"
10. **Synthesize** — "Build Draft Insights"
11. **Verify** — "Protect Citation Integrity"
12. **Deliver** — "Final Report"

# Key Details

**CLI Technology:** "Sourcery is a Node.js CLI — point it at any topic and get a citation-grounded Markdown report in minutes."

**Cost:** "Under $0.10 per report — including residential proxy costs for anti-bot bypass."

**Author:** Built by Nitin Bansal

**Repository:** [github.com/freakynit/sourcery](https://github.com/freakynit/sourcery)

**SQLite:** No mention of SQLite appears in the provided content.

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board && python3 -c "
import json,re,statistics as st
from collections import Counter
rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open('sourcery.jsonl')]
h2s=[]; seq=Counter(); words=[]; refs=[]
for r in rows:
    a=r['article']
    hh=[m.group(1).strip() for m in re.finditer(r'^## (.+)$',a,re.M)]
    seq[tuple(hh)]+=1
    h2s.append(hh)
    words.append(len(re.findall(r'\w+',a)) if not re.search(r'[一-鿿]',a) else len(re.sub(r'\s','',a)))
    refs.append(len(set(re.findall(r'id=\"reference-(\d+)\"',a))))
print('most common H2 seq count:', seq.most_common(1)[0][1], 'distinct seqs', len(seq))
print('H2 mean', st.mean(len(h) for h in h2s))
def has(name): return sum(1 for h in h2s if any(name.lower() in x.lower() for x in h))
for n in ['Table of Contents','Executive Summary','Key Questions','Core Findings','Contradictions','Deep Analysis','Implications','Future Outlook','Unknowns','Evidence Map','References']:
    print(n, has(n))
print('refs mean', st.mean(refs), 'median', st.median(refs), 'max', max(refs))
"

input.command: cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board && python3 - <<'EOF' import json,re,statistics as st from collections import defaultdict rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open('sourcery.jsonl')] def wc(t): zh=len(re.findall(r'[一-鿿]',t)); en=len(re.findall(r'[A-Za-z][A-Za-z\'-]*',t)) return zh+en secw=defaultdict(list); fo=0; tables=0; zh=0; tot=[] cited=0; sent=0; multi=0; cs=0 cfsub=[] for r in rows: a=r['article']; tot.append(wc(a)); if '|' in a and re.search(r'\|.*\|',a): tables+=1 if re.search(r'[一-鿿]{50}',a): zh+=1 for p in re.split(r'^## ',a,flags=re.M)[1:]: name=re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z& ]','',p.split('\n')[0]).strip() body='\n'.join(p.split('\n')[1:]) if name=='References': continue secw[name].append(wc(re.sub(r'<[^>]+>','',body))) if name=='Future Outlook' and re.search(r'Optimistic',p) and re.search(r'Pessimistic',p): fo+=1 if name=='Core Findings': for sub in re.split(r'^### ',body,flags=re.M)[1:]: srcs=set(re.findall(r'#reference-(\d+)',sub))|set(re.findall(r'\[(\d{1,2})\]',re.sub(r']*>.*?','',sub,flags=re.S))) cfsub.append(len(srcs)) # sentence coverage over prose (strip tables/headings) txt=re.sub(r'^\|.*$','',a,flags=re.M); txt=re.sub(r'^#+.*$','',txt,flags=re.M) txt=re.sub(r'^- \[.*$','',txt,flags=re.M) for s in re.split(r'(?<=[.!?。！？])\s+|\n',txt): s=s.strip() if wc(s)<5: continue sent+=1 ids=set(re.findall(r'#reference-(\d+)',s))|set(re.findall(r'\[(\d{1,2})\]',re.sub(r']*>.*?','',s,flags=re.S))) if ids: cited+=1 if len(ids)>=2: multi+=1 print('words mean',round(st.mean(tot)),'tables',tables,'zh',zh,'FO scenarios',fo) print('sent',sent,'coverage %',round(100*cited/sent,1),'multi-source of cited %',round(100*multi/cited,1)) print('CF subsections',len(cfsub),'mean distinct srcs',round(st.mean(cfsub),2),'>=3:',round(100*sum(1 for x in cfsub if x>=3)/len(cfsub)),'single:',round(100*sum(1 for x in cfsub if x==1)/len(cfsub))) for k in ['Core Findings','Deep Analysis','Contradictions & Debates','Key Questions Answered','Evidence Map','Executive Summary','Future Outlook','Implications','Unknowns & Open Questions']: v=secw[k]; print(f'{k:28} n={len(v):3} meanw={round(st.mean(v)) if v else 0}') EOF

input.prompt: List all top-level (H2) section headings in order, verbatim. Also note whether Future Outlook has Optimistic/Base Case/Pessimistic subsections, whether there is an Evidence Map table, and how citations are rendered (anchors like [1] linking to #reference-1?).
Is any architecture/pipeline described? Quote any technical claims. Who is the author/company?

content.content:
# SourceryIntel Analysis

## Company/Author
**SourceryIntel** — a research organization publishing autonomous investigation reports. No individual author is identified; the byline is institutional.

## Architecture/Pipeline Description
No technical architecture is explicitly described. However, the methodology section outlines their research approach:

**Research Process:**
- "Each piece starts from a live strategic question and follows its consequences across domains."
- Reports incorporate "primary material, current developments, skeptical viewpoints, and historical context"
- Claims are "shaped from extracted notes, claims, figures, and source-level observations"
- Work follows "citation hygiene" with bounded expansion to maintain coherence

## Technical Claims
The most explicit technical claim about their operation:

> "Structured evidence, not loose summarization" — they extract and organize source-level data rather than producing generic synthesis.

The organization frames itself as delivering "Autonomous Research Intelligence" with "Every claim, sourced," but provides no details about computational systems, data pipelines, or automated research infrastructure underlying this work.

# Analysis of Deep Research Report Structure

## Top-Level (H2) Section Headings in Order

1. Executive Summary
2. Key Questions Answered
3. Core Findings
4. Contradictions & Debates
5. Deep Analysis
6. Implications
7. Future Outlook
8. Unknowns & Open Questions
9. Evidence Map

## Future Outlook Structure

Yes, the "Future Outlook" section contains three subsections:
- Optimistic Scenario
- Base Case
- Pessimistic Scenario

Each includes a "Probability assessment" statement.

## Evidence Map

Yes, the report includes an "Evidence Map" section featuring a structured table with columns for Theme, Sources, Strength, Key Evidence, and Key Limitation. This table systematizes the evidentiary foundation across ~18 distinct topics (financial losses, attack volume, organizational preparedness, detection limits, etc.).

## Citation Rendering

Citations use anchor links formatted as `[#]` (e.g., `[3]`) connecting to numbered references at the end. These resolve to `#reference-3` style anchors. The References section lists 19 sources with full URLs.

EM with table: 99 3 | Theme | Sources | Strength | Key Gaps 2 | Theme | Sources Supporting | Evidence Strength | Key Gaps 2 | Topic | Sources | Confidence | Notes 2 | Theme | Sources Supporting | Strength | Key Gaps 1 | 研究问题 | Source 1 | Source 2 | Source 3 | Source 4 | Source 5 | Source 6 | Source 7 | Source 8 | Source 9 | Source 10 1 | 维度 | [1] Adda247 | [2] Insurance Business Mag | [3] IAIS GIMAR |

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board && python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re
rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open('sourcery.jsonl')]
n=0; strength=0; gap=0; distinct=set(); triad=0
for r in rows:
    for p in re.split(r'^## ',r['article'],flags=re.M)[1:]:
        if p.startswith('Evidence Map'):
            m=re.search(r'^\|(.+)\|\s*$',p,re.M)
            if not m: continue
            h=m.group(1).lower(); n+=1; distinct.add(h)
            if re.search(r'strength|confiden|强|力度|强度',h): strength+=1
            if re.search(r'gap|limitation|absent|缺口|空白|不足',h): gap+=1
            if re.search(r'strong',h) and re.search(r'moderate|weak',h): triad+=1
print('EM tables',n,'distinct header rows',len(distinct),'has strength col',strength,'has gap col',gap,'strong/mod/weak triad',triad)
EOF

content.text:
Verification complete. Primary sources fetched live (2026-07-13), all 100 artifacts re-analyzed with independent scripts.

**What I confirmed from primary source (sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site, fetched today):**
- 12 stages verbatim: Understand / Plan / Generate / Search / Clean / Extract / Expand / Cross-check ("Find Contradictions") / Remember / Synthesize ("Build Draft Insights") / Verify ("Protect Citation Integrity") / Deliver. Node.js CLI, "Under $0.10 per report — including residential proxy costs", built by Nitin Bansal.
- about.html verbatim: "SQLite persistence — all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory) stored locally"; "Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list"; "LLM-agnostic — works with any OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint".
- github.com/freakynit/sourcery → HTTP 404 (confirmed by curl).
- Identity link holds: the public report the-coming-ai-cybersecurity-crisis.pagey.site has the identical H2 sequence, Optimistic/Base Case/Pessimistic triad, Evidence Map table, and #reference-N anchors.

**What broke under re-measurement (`/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/sourcery.jsonl`):**
- The bare-citation anomaly is NOT confined to one section. Unknowns is 93.9% bare (876 bare / 57 anchored, 90 reports), but **Implications is 42.2% bare (736 bare / 1007 anchored, in 67/100 reports)** and Executive Summary 12%. "Every other section uses HTML anchors" is false.
- Evidence Map schema is free-form: **94 distinct header rows across 99 tables**. The claimed "Theme × Strongest / Moderate / Weak-or-Absent Evidence" appears in only **7/99**. 72 have some strength column, 34 a gaps column.
- Section presence is 99/100 for Contradictions, Unknowns, Deep Analysis, Implications, Future Outlook (the "96/95" figures came from exact-string matching that missed the `&`-vs-`and` variants).
- The task-72 showcase sentence cites `[8]` only — a single source — so it does not demonstrate "the relation itself is cited to both sides."
- Sentence coverage 65.7% / multi-source 40% did not reproduce (I get 54.9% / 29.7%); segmentation-dependent, directionally right, magnitude unverified.

**Where the inference line is crossed:** "Synthesis operates on claim OBJECTS and their RELATIONS, not on raw retrieved text" is stated as documented design but the vendor says only "builds draft insights across **evidence chunks**" — which if anything suggests text chunks. Same for "separate first-class tables", "sections are generated and stitched separately", and "the composer sees a numbered SOURCE CARD POOL": all are artifact inference, not documentation. The repo is private, so none of it is checkable.

input.reasoning:
IDENTITY + PUBLISHED PIPELINE: CONFIRMED from primary source, fetched live 2026-07-13. https://sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site/ states the 12 stages verbatim (Understand, Plan, Generate, Search, Clean, Extract, Expand, Cross-check "Find Contradictions", Remember, Synthesize "Build Draft Insights", Verify "Protect Citation Integrity", Deliver), "Sourcery is a Node.js CLI", "Under $0.10 per report — including residential proxy costs for anti-bot bypass", built by Nitin Bansal, repo github.com/freakynit/sourcery. about.html states verbatim: "SQLite persistence — all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory) stored locally"; "Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list"; "Sourcery doesn't smooth over conflicts; contradictions between sources are surfaced explicitly"; "LLM-agnostic — works with any OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint". curl confirms github.com/freakynit/sourcery = HTTP 404. The public report the-coming-ai-cybersecurity-crisis.pagey.site returns the identical H2 scaffold (Executive Summary > Key Questions Answered > Core Findings > Contradictions & Debates > Deep Analysis > Implications > Future Outlook [Optimistic/Base Case/Pessimistic + probability assessment] > Unknowns & Open Questions > Evidence Map, 19 refs, [N] -> #reference-N), which does pin the leaderboard entry to this system. sourceryintel.com is marketing-only as claimed.

ARTIFACT STATS I REPRODUCED (scripts over drb_corpus/gpt55_board/sourcery.jsonl, n=100): 86/100 identical H2 order (14 distinct orders total); 11.0 H2 mean; refs mean 11.91, median 12, max 19; tables 100/100; Future Outlook triad 99/99; 50/50 Chinese reports keep English H2 (48/50 strictly; 2 have some CJK H2); Core Findings anchored 14,904 vs 335 bare; "Source N" leak in 65 reports / 1,601 occurrences (claim said 64/1,470 — close); Contradictions & Debates mean 6.63 named tensions; Core Findings mean 4.74 distinct sources per subsection (claim: 4.94), 70% cite >=3 (claim 74%), 12% single-source (claim 15%); section word ordering Core Findings (~42% of body) >> Deep Analysis > Contradictions > Key Questions > rest. Heading inconsistency "Contradictions & Debates" (96) vs "Contradictions and Debates" (3) confirmed, i.e. no global normalization pass.

WHAT IS WRONG OR OVERSTATED:
1) FALSE as stated: "'Unknowns & Open Questions' uses bare [N] while EVERY OTHER SECTION uses HTML anchors." Unknowns is 93.9% bare (876 bare / 57 anchored, in 90/100 reports), but Implications is 42.2% bare (736 bare / 1,007 anchored, in 67/100 reports) and Executive Summary is 12% bare (243/1,786, 34 reports). The linkification anomaly is graded across sections, not a single-section miss. The multi-pass inference survives (arguably strengthened), but the stated fact does not.
2) FALSE as stated: Evidence Map "always a table: Theme x Strongest Evidence / Moderate Evidence / Weak-or-Absent Evidence". It is always a table (99/99), but there are 94 DISTINCT header rows across those 99 tables; only 7/99 use a Strong/Moderate/Weak triad; 72 have any strength/confidence column, 34 a gaps column. The Evidence Map schema is essentially free-form per report — which cuts against the "fixed schema" framing at table level.
3) Wrong counts: Contradictions, Unknowns, Deep Analysis, Implications and Future Outlook each appear in 99/100 reports, not 96/96/95. (The claim's own text is internally inconsistent here: section 1 says 100/100 core set, 4c says 99/100, the artifact bullet says 96/95.)
4) Example in 4(b) does not support 4(b): the task-72 sentence "The ratio between the most conservative and most optimistic estimates exceeds 10:1, driven primarily by divergent assumptions about task coverage, diffusion speed, and complementary investment rather than conflicting empirical data" cites [8] and only [8] (twice). It is a cross-source-computed claim, but it is NOT "cited to both sides".
5) Unverified magnitudes: sentence-level coverage 65.7% (n=17,160) and 40% multi-source cited sentences did not reproduce under my segmentation (54.9% coverage over n=31,008; 29.7% of cited sentences have >=2 sources). Direction (coverage well under 100%; a large minority of cited sentences are multi-source) holds; the exact figures are method-dependent and should not be quoted as measured constants without publishing the tokenizer/splitter. Same for the 11,910-word mean (I get 10,338 with a CJK-char + Latin-word counter) and the per-section word budgets — ordering reproduces, absolute values are tokenizer-dependent.

WHERE THE INFERENCE/DOCUMENTATION LINE IS CROSSED (the main methodological problem):
- "LOAD-BEARING FACT: ... sources / claims / contradictions are separate first-class SQLite tables. ... Synthesis then operates on claim OBJECTS and their RELATIONS, not on raw retrieved text." Only the first half is documented. The site says state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory) is stored in SQLite — it does NOT say separate first-class tables, and it does NOT say synthesis reads claim objects. The vendor's own words for stage 10 are "Build Draft Insights" / "builds draft insights across EVIDENCE CHUNKS" — "evidence chunks" is if anything evidence AGAINST a pure claim-object synthesis and for chunk-level text. This is presented as documented design and is not; the repo is 404/private so it cannot be checked at all. Cross-check (8) running BEFORE Synthesize (10) IS documented and is confirmed.
- Section 4's entire composition model (schema-driven, section-scoped composition against a claim store; "sections are generated and stitched separately, then a deterministic citation-rebuild pass runs over them"; "proof the composer sees a numbered SOURCE CARD POOL") is inference from output text stated as fact. The vendor publishes NOTHING about report structure, section templates, or per-section generation. The one piece with vendor backing is the deterministic citation rebuild ("deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list") — that one is documented. The "Source N" prose leak is strong artifact evidence for a numbered source pool, but "proof" is too strong: it is consistent with numbered sources being injected into a single-pass prompt too.
- Section 5 ("no global revision/critique loop") is a reasonable negative inference from surviving heading inconsistencies and the linkification gap, but it is an inference; the Verify stage exists and is documented, and absence of a prose-critique loop is not documented anywhere.

NET: the vendor pipeline, cost, stack, SQLite state contents, deterministic citation rebuild, contradiction surfacing and stage ORDER are CONFIRMED from primary source. The corpus-level structural statistics are CONFIRMED (with three specific numbers wrong). The "claim database, not document buffer / synthesis over claim objects and relations" load-bearing framing and the whole section-scoped composition model are INFERENCE presented as documented design, and two supporting statistics (single-section bare-cite anomaly; fixed Evidence Map schema) are false as written.
input.corrected: SYSTEM: sourcery (rank 6, 51.17) = "Sourcery", an independent Node.js deep-research CLI by Nitin Bansal (GitHub @freakynit). Identity CONFIRMED: public Sourcery reports at *.pagey.site use the same 11-section scaffold, the same Optimistic/Base Case/Pessimistic triad and the same [N] -> #reference-N anchor mechanics as our 100 benchmark artifacts. Not the Python tool sourcery.ai. HAS PAPER: false. Repo github.com/freakynit/sourcery is announced on the product site but returns HTTP 404 (private) as of 2026-07-13, so NO code-level claim about Sourcery can be verified. Everything below is split into (A) vendor-documented and (B) inferred-from-artifacts. (A) VENDOR-DOCUMENTED (primary source: sourcery-deep-research.pagey.site + /about.html, fetched 2026-07-13; quotes verbatim) - 12-stage pipeline: 1 Understand, 2 Plan, 3 Generate, 4 Search, 5 Clean, 6 Extract, 7 Expand, 8 Cross-check ("Find Contradictions"), 9 Remember, 10 Synthesize ("Build Draft Insights"), 11 Verify ("Protect Citation Integrity"), 12 Deliver. - "Sourcery is a Node.js CLI"; "Under $0.10 per report — including residential proxy costs for anti-bot bypass"; "LLM-agnostic — works with any OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint". - "SQLite persistence — all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory) stored locally". - "Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list." - "Sourcery doesn't smooth over conflicts; contradictions between sources are surfaced explicitly and carried into the final report." - Stage ORDER is the one architecturally load-bearing documented fact: contradiction detection (8) precedes synthesis (10). Persisted state includes claims and contradictions as named entities. (B) INFERRED FROM THE 100 ARTIFACTS — labelled as inference, NOT documented - INFERRED (not documented): that claims/sources/contradictions are separate first-class TABLES, and that synthesis operates on claim OBJECTS AND RELATIONS rather than on retrieved text. The vendor's own wording for stage 10 is "builds draft insights across EVIDENCE CHUNKS", which is at least as consistent with chunk/text-level synthesis. Do not state the claim-object synthesis as documented design. - INFERRED (strong): a fixed global report template, genre-invariant. MEASURED: 86/100 identical H2 order (14 distinct orders); 100/100 have TOC + Executive Summary + Key Questions Answered + Core Findings + References; 99/100 have Contradictions & Debates, Deep Analysis, Implications, Future Outlook, Unknowns & Open Questions, Evidence Map. Future Outlook = Optimistic/Base Case/Pessimistic in 99/99. All 50 Chinese-language reports keep ENGLISH H2 headings (48/50 strictly). - MEASURED retrieval profile: 11.9 refs/report (median 12, max 19) against ~10-12k words/report — few sources, mined very deeply. Task 72 self-reports "synthesizes 18 English-language academic sources" and its reference list does contain exactly 18. - MEASURED citation mechanics: body cites are HTML anchors [N]; zero raw URLs in body. The deterministic rebuild is vendor-documented. - INFERRED (multi-pass / section-scoped generation): citation FORMAT varies by section, but NOT as a single-section miss. Unknowns & Open Questions is 93.9% bare [N] (876 bare / 57 anchored, 90/100 reports); Implications is 42.2% bare (736 / 1,007, 67/100 reports); Executive Summary 12% bare; Core Findings 2.2% bare (335 / 14,904); Future Outlook and Evidence Map <1% bare. The linkification/verify pass degrades unevenly ACROSS sections, which is consistent with (but not proof of) per-section generation + a post-hoc citation rebuild. - MEASURED source-card leak: 65/100 reports leak internal source labels into prose ("Source 4 documents...", 1,601 occurrences). Strongly suggests (does not prove) the composer sees a numbered source-card pool. - MEASURED thematic outline: Core Findings subsections draw mean 4.74 distinct sources (70% cite >=3; 12% single-source) => writing units are themes, not per-source summaries. - MEASURED three synthesis-shaped sections: Contradictions & Debates (99/100; mean 6.63 named tensions, each stating thesis vs antithesis with cites and then resolving); Unknowns & Open Questions (99/100, numbered gaps grounded in what sources fail to cover); Evidence Map (99/99 always a table). CORRECTION: the Evidence Map table has NO fixed schema — 94 distinct header rows across 99 tables; only 7/99 use Strongest/Moderate/Weak columns; 72 have some strength/confidence column and 34 a gaps column. Do not claim a fixed "Theme x Strongest/Moderate/Weak-or-Absent" schema. - MEASURED partial sentence-level grounding: sentence-level citation coverage is well below 100% (65.7% by the original splitter, 54.9% by an independent one) and a large minority of cited sentences carry >=2 distinct sources (40% original, 29.7% independent). Both are segmentation-dependent; report as "roughly one third to one half of sentences are uncited connective/interpretive prose" rather than as exact constants. NOTE: the showcased task-72 cross-source sentence ("...ratio ... exceeds 10:1 ... driven primarily by divergent assumptions about task coverage, diffusion speed, and complementary investment rather than conflicting empirical data") is cited to [8] ONLY, so it does not demonstrate a relation cited to both sides. - INFERRED (negative): no global revision pass over the finished draft — "Contradictions & Debates" (96) vs "Contradictions and Debates" (3) heading variants and the uneven linkification survive to output. The documented "critique" is upstream (Cross-check over claims); absence of a downstream prose-critique loop is inferred, not documented.
